memes.label: Aleksandr Kirsánof
memes.description: Medical professor, experimental scientist, and Viéra Pavlovna's second husband — the character who most consistently applies the theory of egotism to practical life, exemplifying the novel's ideal of "coldly calculating common sense"

memes.label: Beaumont's Return
memes.description: The return of Charles Beaumont to Petersburg — revealed to be Lopukhóf, alive and prosperous, having spent years in America — the event that brings the novel's central plot to its harmonious resolution

memes.label: "Part VI: Change of Scenes"
memes.description: "Change of Scenes"

memes.label: Common Sense
memes.description: The "new people's" term for practical rationality — the application of clear, self-aware reasoning to life decisions, contrasted with the romantic illusions, social conventions, and sentimental morality of the old order

memes.label: Crystal Palace
memes.description: The enormous edifice of cast-iron and glass that appears in Viéra Pavlovna's Fourth Dream — Chernyshevsky's architectural metaphor for a future socialist society based on cooperation, abundance, and the transformation of human relations

memes.label: Dmitri Lopukhóf
memes.description: Medical student, materialist philosopher, and [[Viéra Pavlovna]]'s first husband — the character who introduces the [[Theory of Egotism|theory of egotism]] and orchestrates Viéra's escape through a [[Fictitious Marriage|fictitious marriage]], later faking his own death to free her

memes.label: Establishment of the Sewing Cooperative
memes.description: Viéra Pavlovna's founding of the sewing cooperative — beginning with three seamstresses and a simple profit-sharing arrangement, growing over three years into a thriving enterprise that embodies the "new people's" economic principles

memes.label: Fictitious Marriage
memes.description: The strategic use of legal marriage as a fiction to circumvent patriarchal constraints — Lopukhóf and Viéra Pavlovna's marriage is a legal form that enables her escape from her parents' household while preserving her independence

memes.label: Gorokhovaïa Street
memes.description: The street in St. Petersburg where the Rozálsky family lives — the site of Viéra Pavlovna's childhood "cellar" and the world of the old order from which she must escape

memes.label: Gostinui Dvor
memes.description: The great merchants' arcade of St. Petersburg — a site of commerce and social exchange that represents the economic world the "new people" navigate and transform

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: The central ethical goal of Chernyshevsky's philosophy — happiness understood not as passive pleasure but as the active satisfaction that comes from rational [[Self-Cultivation|self-development]], useful work, and relations of mutual respect between free individuals

memes.label: Julie
memes.description: A Frenchwoman who befriends Viéra Pavlovna and introduces her to a wider social circle — a sympathetic figure from outside the Russian social structure who provides Viéra with connections and perspective

memes.label: Katerina Vasílyevna
memes.description: Daughter of the wealthy contractor Pólozof and close friend of Viéra Pavlovna — her correspondence with Viéra provides one of the novel's most explicit articulations of the "new people's" philosophy of love and autonomy

memes.label: Katerina's Wedding
memes.description: The marriage of Katerina Vasílyevna Pólozova to Charles Beaumont — the union that extends the "new people's" circle and completes the novel's pattern of marriages founded on rational choice and mutual respect

memes.label: Kirsánof's Hospital
memes.description: The hospital where Aleksandr Kirsánof conducts his medical research and treats patients — a space where the "new people's" scientific ethos is practiced and where Kirsánof's dual identity as researcher and healer is enacted

memes.label: Labor as Vocation
memes.description: The idea that work should be chosen for its intrinsic satisfaction rather than merely for income — a central principle of the "new people's" economic philosophy, modeled by Lopukhóf's choice of medical research over private practice and Viéra's management of the cooperative as a "hobby"

memes.label: Lopukhóf's Fake Suicide
memes.description: Lopukhóf's elaborately staged suicide — traveling to Riazan, leaving his clothes by the river, and vanishing — the mechanism by which he dissolves his legal marriage to Viéra while preserving her freedom and his own dignity

memes.label: Marya Alekséyevna
memes.description: Viéra Pavlovna's mother — a domineering, pragmatic woman of the old order who treats her daughter as a commodity to be sold in marriage, yet whose harsh realism contains a distorted recognition of the same truths that the "new people" articulate more clearly

memes.label: Marya Alekséyevna's Downfall
memes.description: The collapse of Marya Alekséyevna's scheme to marry Viéra to Storeshnikof — the moment when the old order's logic of commodification is defeated by the "new people's" philosophy of autonomy

memes.label: Massachusetts
memes.description: The American state where Lopukhóf/Beaumont establishes himself — representing in the novel's geography the rural alternative to urban life, associated with the Fourierist communities and utopian experiments of nineteenth-century America

memes.label: Medical-Surgical Academy Hospital
memes.description: The St. Petersburg military hospital where Dmitri Lopukhóf serves as surgeon and later teaches — a center of the novel's medical subculture, where young doctors devote themselves to observation and research rather than private practice

memes.label: Mertsálof
memes.description: A priest and friend of Lopukhóf who performs Viéra's wedding ceremony and remains part of the "new people's" circle — representing the possibility of religious office reconciled with rationalist philosophy

memes.label: Moscow
memes.description: Russia's former capital, mentioned in the novel as a point of contrast and connection — the city to which Lopukhóf travels on his way to staging his suicide, and a symbol of the broader Russian world beyond Petersburg

memes.label: New York
memes.description: The American city where Lopukhóf lives during his years of absence — representing the New World as a site of freedom and transformation, the place where a Russian can shed his old identity and become someone new

memes.label: Nástenka
memes.description: A young woman whose story Kirsánof recounts — a factory worker seduced and abandoned by Sólovtsof, whose narrative serves as a cautionary example of the old order's treatment of women and an illustration of Kirsánof's compassionate practice

memes.label: Nástenka's Confession
memes.description: Kirsánof's recounting of Nástenka Kryukova's story to Viéra Pavlovna — the tale of a factory worker seduced and abandoned by Sólovtsof, serving as a cautionary example of the old order's treatment of vulnerable women

memes.label: Opera and Culture
memes.description: The role of art — particularly opera and music — in the self-development of the "new people," representing the cultivation of sensibility that distinguishes a fully developed human being from one who merely survives

memes.label: Pável Konstantinuitch
memes.description: Viéra Pavlovna's father — a minor government official whose passivity and ineffectuality make him complicit in his wife Marya Alekséyevna's domination of their household

memes.label: Pólozof
memes.description: A wealthy government contractor and father of Katerina Vasílyevna — a representative of the old order's commercial class whose fortune is built on the same system of exploitation that the "new people" seek to transcend

memes.label: Pólozof's Estate
memes.description: The country estate of the wealthy government contractor Pólozof — representing the old order's concentration of wealth in land and the economic system that the "new people's" cooperative principles are designed to transcend

memes.label: Pólozof's Stearine Factory
memes.description: The stearine factory in which Pólozof holds shares and serves as manager — a site of capitalist production and financial speculation whose sale to the London firm of Hodgson, Lotter and Company connects the novel's economic world to international commerce

memes.label: Rakhmétof
memes.description: The "extraordinary man" of the novel — a revolutionary ascetic who has transformed himself through rigorous self-discipline into an instrument of social change, presented as a higher type than the "new people" but not a model for ordinary readers

memes.label: Rakhmétof's Visit
memes.description: Rakhmétof's visit to Viéra Pavlovna on the evening of Lopukhóf's disappearance — delivering Lopukhóf's letter and explaining, with characteristic harshness and hidden tenderness, why the suicide was necessary

memes.label: Rational Calculation
memes.description: The deliberative process by which the "new people" apply the theory of egotism to practical decisions — weighing competing interests to determine the course of action that maximizes genuine satisfaction, including the satisfaction of acting nobly

memes.label: The Rozálsky Apartment
memes.description: The apartment on Gorokhovaïa Street where Viéra Pavlovna grows up — the "cellar" of her First Dream, a domestic prison ruled by Marya Alekséyevna's commodifying logic

memes.label: Sadovïa Street
memes.description: A St. Petersburg street that appears in the novel's urban geography — part of the network of streets, bridges, and districts that map the "new people's" movements through the imperial capital

memes.label: Science and Progress
memes.description: The role of natural science — particularly medicine — as a model for rational thought and social progress, embodied in Kirsánof's research and the novel's broader faith in scientific understanding as the foundation for human improvement

memes.label: Self-Cultivation
memes.description: The deliberate cultivation of one's intellectual and moral capacities through reading, observation, and reflection — presented in the novel as both the path to happiness and the duty of every rational person

memes.label: Semyonovsky Bridge
memes.description: A bridge in St. Petersburg that appears at key moments in the novel — a liminal space associated with transitions, decisions, and the boundary between the old order and the new

memes.label: Serzh
memes.description: An officer friend of Storeshnikof who participates in the scheme to seduce Viéra — a minor figure of the old order's masculine world of bets, dares, and predatory courtship

memes.label: Sewing Cooperative
memes.description: The sewing workshop founded by Viéra Pavlovna as a practical experiment in cooperative labor — a model of economic organization based on profit-sharing, mutual aid, and worker self-management

memes.label: Sewing Cooperative (Union)
memes.description: The sewing cooperative founded and managed by Viéra Pavlovna — a model enterprise of egalitarian labor organization, profit-sharing, and worker self-governance that embodies the novel's vision of economic emancipation

memes.label: St. Petersburg
memes.description: The primary setting of What Is to Be Done? — the Russian imperial capital where Viéra Pavlovna's story unfolds, representing both the old order's social structures and the emerging world of the "new people"

memes.label: Storeshnikof
memes.description: The wealthy young man whom Marya Alekséyevna schemes to marry to Viéra — a representative of the old order's marriage market, whose vapid character illustrates everything the "new people" reject

memes.label: Sólovtsof
memes.description: A cynical seducer who abandons Nástenka Kryukova after promising marriage — the novel's most vivid example of the old order's predatory treatment of women

memes.label: The Kirsánofs' Apartment
memes.description: The apartment shared by Viéra Pavlovna and Aleksandr Kirsánof after their marriage — the domestic space of a relationship based on mutual love rather than legal fiction, where work, companionship, and the "new people's" social life continue

memes.label: The Lopukhófs' Apartment
memes.description: The apartment shared by Viéra Pavlovna and Dmitri Lopukhóf after their marriage — a domestic space designed around the principle of autonomy, with separate bedrooms connected by "neutral rooms" that spatialize the "new people's" philosophy of relationships

memes.label: The New Woman
memes.description: The social type represented by Viéra Pavlovna — a woman who works, studies, manages enterprises, chooses her partners freely, and refuses to be defined by her relations to men, presented not as an exception but as a model of what all women can become

memes.label: The Sapient Reader
memes.description: The fictional interlocutor whom the narrator repeatedly addresses in satirical asides — a composite figure representing the conventional literary expectations and moral prejudices that Chernyshevsky's novel deliberately frustrates

memes.label: The Sapient Reader's Lesson
memes.description: The narrator's extended address to the "sapient reader" at the end of the Rakhmétof chapter — a direct intervention that reframes the entire novel as a democratic argument about human potential
aliases: the colloquy with the sapient reader

memes.label: The Sewing Workshop
memes.description: The physical space of Viéra Pavlovna's sewing cooperative — a workshop that is also a school, a bank, and a community, embodying in its spatial arrangement the principles of cooperative labor and mutual education

memes.label: The Temptation of Hamlet
memes.description: The pivotal conversation in Part I, Chapter VIII between Lopukhóf and Viéra Pavlovna in which the theory of egotism is first fully articulated — the intellectual event that founds the novel's philosophical structure

memes.label: Theory of Egotism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine central to What Is to Be Done? — that all human actions, including apparently selfless ones, are governed by calculated self-interest, and that this principle, properly understood, leads to rational and noble conduct

memes.label: True Love
memes.description: The novel's distinction between "true love" — a freely given, mutually enriching relationship between autonomous equals — and "erotic selfishness" or "erotic fancy," which treats the beloved as a possession

memes.label: Vasilyevsky Island
memes.description: The St. Petersburg district where the Lopukhófs and Kirsánofs live — the geography of the "new people," characterized by apartments with "neutral rooms" and spaces designed for intellectual companionship and personal autonomy

memes.label: Viéra Pavlovna's Dreams
memes.description: The four allegorical dreams experienced by Viéra Pavlovna that structure the philosophical and psychological development of the novel, moving from personal liberation through social analysis to utopian vision

memes.label: Viéra's First Dream
memes.description: Viéra's first allegorical dream — she is locked in a damp cellar, then liberated into a field, symbolizing her awakening from the prison of her parents' household to the possibility of freedom

memes.label: Viéra's Fourth Dream
memes.description: Viéra's culminating allegorical dream — a vision of a transformed society where men and women live in cooperation and abundance, centered on an enormous palace of cast-iron and glass

memes.label: Viéra's Marriage to Kirsánof
memes.description: Viéra Pavlovna's marriage to Aleksandr Kirsánof after Lopukhóf's disappearance — a union of genuine mutual love that embodies the novel's ideal of a relationship between fully autonomous equals

memes.label: Viéra's Marriage to Lopukhóf
memes.description: The legal marriage between Viéra Pavlovna and Dmitri Lopukhóf — a "fictitious" union designed not as a romantic consummation but as a legal instrument to liberate Viéra from her parents' household

memes.label: Viéra's Second Dream
memes.description: Viéra's second allegorical dream — Lopukhóf and Mertsálof walk across a field discussing the allegory of "actual dirt" and "putrid dirt," representing the materialist understanding of human nature as the product of social conditions

memes.label: Viéra's Third Dream
memes.description: Viéra's third dream — beginning with thoughts of the opera singer Bosio and moving into a deeper exploration of her own desires, marking the transition from passive recipient of others' ideas to active, self-aware consciousness

memes.label: Viéra Pavlovna
memes.description: The protagonist of What Is to Be Done? — a young woman who escapes her family's "cellar" through a [[Fictitious Marriage|fictitious marriage]], founds a [[Sewing Cooperative|sewing cooperative]], experiences four allegorical [[Viéras Dreams|dreams]], and embodies the novel's ideal of the emancipated "[[The New Woman|new woman]]"

memes.label: Women's Independence
memes.description: The principle that women must achieve economic, intellectual, and emotional independence — a central theme of the novel embodied in Viéra Pavlovna's journey from her parents' "cellar" to a life of autonomous work, love, and self-development
